corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: Zero-shot CLIP outperforms this baseline slightly more of-40 30 20 10 0 10 20 30 40 Score (%) Zero-Shot CLIP vs.

Across a 27 dataset eval suite, a zero-shot CLIP classifier outperforms a fully supervised linear classifier fitted on ResNet-50 features on 16 datasets, including ImageNet. ten than not and wins on 16 of the 27 datasets.

On two of these datasets, Stanford Cars and Food101, zero-shot CLIP outperforms logistic regression on ResNet-50 features by over 20% while on two others, Flowers102 and FGVCAircraft, zero-shot CLIP underperforms by over 10%.
On STL10, CLIP achieves 99.3% overall which appears to be a new state of the art despite not using any training examples.
On STL10, CLIP achieves 99.3% overall which appears to be a new state of the art despite not using any training examples.
Zeroshot CLIP significantly outperforms a ResNet-50 on two datasets measuring action recognition in videos.

That a BiT-M ResNet-152x2 performs best in a 16-shot setting is somewhat surprising since, as analyzed in Section 3.2, the Noisy Student EfficientNet-L2 outperforms it in a fully supervised setting by almost 5% on average across 27 datasets.

While it achieves near 100% accuracy on its proxy training task and manual inspection + threshold tuning results in very high precision with good recall among the found nearest-neighbors, we can not tractably check its recall across 400 million examples.
Many complex tasks and visual concepts can be difficult to specify just through text.
Future work is needed to develop methods that combine CLIP's strong zero-shot performance with efficient few-shot learning.
On Kinet-ics700, CLIP outperforms a ResNet-50 by 14.5%.
Zeroshot CLIP also outperforms a ResNet-50's features by 7.7% on UCF101.
For the first, we use ResNet-50 as the base architecture for the image encoder due to its widespread adoption and proven performance.
For the second architecture, we experiment with the recently introduced Vision Transformer (ViT) .
The text encoder is a Transformer with the architecture modifications described in .

While previous computer vision research has often scaled models by increasing the width or depth in isolation, for the ResNet image encoders we adapt the approach of which found that allocating additional compute across all of width, depth, and resolution outperforms only allocating it to only one dimension of the model. tune the ratio of compute allocated to each dimension for their EfficientNet architecture, we use a simple baseline of allocating additional compute equally to increasing the width, depth, and resolution of the model.
LM RN50 This is a multimodal model that uses an autoregressive loss instead of a contrastive loss, while using the ResNet-50 architecture as in the smallest contrastive model.
Apart from the training objective, the model was trained on the same dataset for the same number of epochs as other CLIP models.
After filtering to keep only images with natural language titles and/or descriptions in English, the dataset shrunk by a factor of 6 to only 15 million photos.
To address this, we constructed a new dataset of 400 million (image, text) pairs collected form a variety of publicly available sources on the Internet.
The resulting dataset has a similar total word count as the WebText dataset used to train GPT-2.

Across a 27 dataset eval suite, a zero-shot CLIP classifier outperforms a fully supervised linear classifier fitted on ResNet-50 features on 16 datasets, including ImageNet. ten than not and wins on 16 of the 27 datasets.

In order to avoid training thousands of linear classifiers, we estimate the effective data efficiency based on a loglinear interpolation of the performance of a 1, 2, 4, 8, 16shot (when possible), and a fully supervised linear classifier trained on each dataset.

Performance varies widely from still underperforming a one-shot classifier on two datasets to matching an estimated 184 labeled examples per class. have widely varying efficiency per dataset from less than 1 labeled example per class to 184.
On all 5 datasets, both zero-shot accuracy and fully supervised accuracy are over 90%.
